title: Why Do Cats Hate Cucumbers? The Surprise Explains Everything 🐱
description: Discover why cats jump in fear when they see a cucumber. A virologist from the University of Oxford explains the feline startle reflex. Is it the cucumber or the scare? Pure science behind the internet's most viral meme.

segments.voice: The internet is full of videos of cats dramatically jumping when they see a cucumber placed behind them. But do they really hate cucumbers? The answer is more complex and remarkable than it seems.

According to Dr. John Bradshaw, an ethologist at the University of Bristol and expert in feline behavior, the reaction isn't to the cucumber specifically, but to the surprise itself. A cat could react just as dramatically to any object placed without warning.

Cats have a highly developed peripheral vision. They remain constantly alert to their surroundings. When something appears suddenly where they didn't expect it, their survival instinct kicks in immediately.
Some researchers suggest that cucumbers might trigger a specific instinct in cats. The elongated shape and green color could vaguely resemble a snake, a natural predator of large felines in the wild.

A 2016 study by researchers at Kyoto University in Japan specifically examined this reaction. They placed different objects behind cats and recorded their responses. Cucumbers, curiously, triggered more reactions than other vegetables.

However, the study concluded that the exaggerated reaction was primarily due to the surprise factor. The cats had been conditioned not to expect anything in that particular space, and the cucumber was just the chosen object.
In 2018, the American Feline Medical Association even warned against this trend of scaring cats with cucumbers. They explained that repeated stress could cause chronic anxiety in the animals.

Cats have a very sensitive nervous system. When startled, they experience a spike in adrenaline and cortisol. Doing this repeatedly isn't playful—it's potentially harmful to their psychological well-being.

The real question is: Is there something specific about cucumbers? Researchers at the University of Edinburgh conducted experiments comparing cucumbers with other shapes and colors. The results were surprising.
When cucumbers were placed gradually, allowing the cat to see them approach, there was no panic reaction. The cucumber's shape itself didn't trigger fear. It was definitely the surprise factor.
An important note: Cats trust their guardians. When you place something unexpected in their space without warning, you're violating that trust. Some cats may develop generalized anxiety as a result.
Dr. Kristyn Vitale, a feline behavior expert at the University of Oregon, studied the stress caused by repeated surprises. Cats exposed to frequent surprises showed elevated cortisol levels for days.
If you really want to test whether your cat is afraid of cucumbers specifically, do it correctly: place a cucumber gradually while your cat can see it. Most cats will ignore it completely.

What IS true is that some cats may associate the smell of cucumbers with unpleasant situations. Historically, fresh cucumbers were used in home remedies to scare cats away from gardens. The smell might have a negative connotation.
But even this is anecdotal. Most cats, when properly introduced to fresh cucumbers, simply sniff them curiously and walk away.
In conclusion, cats don't inherently hate cucumbers. What they dislike is the surprise and the violation of their sense of security. They respect guardians who are predictable.
If you have a cat, please avoid this harmful meme. The videos are entertaining for us, but stressful for them. There are a thousand more fun and safe ways to interact with your cat.
The science of feline behavior teaches us that cats deserve respect and predictability. Now that you know the truth, you can enjoy your cat without causing unnecessary stress.
